TEXT-ALIGN: right" align=right><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>270</FONT></P></TD> <TD style="PADDING-RIGHT: 0in; PADDING-LEFT: 0in; BACKGROUND: #cceeff; PADDING-BOTTOM: 0in; WIDTH: 1%; PADDING-TOP: 0in" vAlign=bottom width="1%" bgColor=#cceeff> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 1pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P></TD></TR></TBODY></TABLE></BODY></HTML> <!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN"> <HTML><HEAD> <META http-equiv=Content-Type content="text/html; charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>11.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>C</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>OMMITMENTS AND</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> C</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ONTINGENCIES</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company continually evaluates contingencies based upon the best available evidence.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company believes that allowances for loss have been provided to the extent necessary and that its assessment of contingencies is reasonable.&nbsp; To the extent that resolution of contingencies results in amounts that vary from the Company&#146;s estimates, future earnings will be charged or credited.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The principal contingencies are described below:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Insurance &#151; </FONT></I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>The Company&#146;s workers&#146; compensation risks are self-insured in certain states. In addition, other workers&#146; compensation risks and certain levels of insured general liability risks are based on retrospective premium plans, deductible plans, and self-insured retention plans.&nbsp; The liability for workers&#146; compensation risks is accounted for on a present value basis.&nbsp; Actual claim settlements and expenses incident thereto may differ from the provisions for loss.&nbsp; Property risks have been underwritten by a subsidiary and are reinsured with unrelated insurance companies.&nbsp; Operating divisions and subsidiaries have paid premiums, and the insurance subsidiary has provided loss allowances, based upon actuarially determined estimates.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Litigation &#151; </FONT></I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>On October&nbsp;6, 2006, the Company petitioned the Tax Court (<I>In Re: Ralphs Grocery Company and Subsidiaries, formerly known as Ralphs Supermarkets,&nbsp;Inc., Docket No.&nbsp;20364-06</I>) for a redetermination of deficiencies set by the Commissioner of Internal Revenue.&nbsp; The dispute at issue involves a 1992 transaction in which Ralphs Holding Company acquired the stock of Ralphs Grocery Company and made an election under Section&nbsp;338(h)(10)&nbsp;of the Internal Revenue Code.&nbsp; The Commissioner has determined that the acquisition of the stock was not a purchase as defined by Section&nbsp;338(h)(3)&nbsp;of the Internal Revenue Code and that the acquisition does not qualify as a purchase.&nbsp; The Company believes that it has strong arguments in favor of its position and believes it is more likely than not that its position will be sustained.&nbsp; However, due to the inherent uncertainty involved in the litigation process, there can be no assurances that the Tax Court will rule&nbsp;in favor of the Company.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, an adverse decision would require a cash payment up to approximately $451.&nbsp; In conjunction with SFAS 141R, any accounting implications of an adverse decision in this case would be charged through the income statement.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>On February&nbsp;2, 2004, the Attorney General for the State of California filed an action in Los Angeles federal court (<I>California, ex rel Lockyer v. Safeway,&nbsp;Inc. dba Vons, a Safeway Company; Albertson&#146;s,&nbsp;Inc. and Ralphs Grocery Company, a division of Horizon Foods Group</I>, United States District Court Central District of California, Case No.&nbsp;CV04-0687) alleging that the Mutual Strike Assistance Agreement (the &#147;Agreement&#148;) between the Company, Albertson&#146;s,&nbsp;Inc. and Safeway Inc. (collectively, the &#147;Retailers&#148;), which was designed to prevent the union from placing disproportionate pressure on one or more of the Retailers by picketing such Retailer(s)&nbsp;but not the other Retailer(s)&nbsp;during the labor dispute in southern California, violated Section&nbsp;1 of the Sherman Act. The lawsuit seeks declarative and injunctive relief. On May&nbsp;28, 2008, pursuant to a stipulation between the parties, the court entered a final judgment in favor of the defendants.&nbsp; As a result of the stipulation and final judgment, there are no further claims to be litigated at the trial court level.&nbsp; The Attorney General has appealed a trial court ruling to the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals and the defendants are appealing a separate ruling.&nbsp; Although this lawsuit is subject to uncertainties inherent to the litigation process, based on the information presently available to the Company, management does not expect that the ultimate resolution of this action will have a material adverse effect on the Company&#146;s financial condition, results of operations or cash flows.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2>Various claims and lawsuits arising in the normal course of business, including suits charging violations of certain antitrust, wage and hour, or civil rights laws, are pending against the Company. Some of these suits purport or have been determined to be class actions and/or seek substantial damages. Any damages that may be awarded in antitrust cases will be automatically trebled. Although it is not possible at this time to evaluate the merits of all of these claims and lawsuits, nor their likelihood of success, the Company is of the belief that any resulting liability will not have a material adverse effect on the Company&#146;s financial position.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company continually evaluates its exposure to loss contingencies arising from pending or threatened litigation and believes it has made adequate provisions therefor. Nonetheless, assessing and predicting the outcomes of these matters involve substantial uncertainties. It remains possible that despite management&#146;s current belief, material differences in actual outcomes or changes in management&#146;s evaluation or predictions could arise that could have a material adverse impact on the Company&#146;s financial condition or results of operation.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Guarantees &#151;</FONT></I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> The Company has guaranteed half of the indebtedness of two real estate entities in which Horizon Foods Group has a 50% ownership interest.&nbsp; The Company&#146;s share of the responsibility for this indebtedness, should the entities be unable to meet their obligations, totals approximately $7.&nbsp; Based on the covenants underlying this indebtedness as of August&nbsp;15, 2009, it is unlikely that the Company will be responsible for repayment of these obligations.&nbsp; The Company also agreed to guarantee, up to $25, the indebtedness of an entity in which Horizon Foods Group has a 50% ownership interest.&nbsp; The Company&#146;s share of the responsibility, as of August&nbsp;15, 2009, should the entity be unable to meet its obligations, totals approximately $25 and is collateralized by approximately $5 of inventory located in the Company&#146;s stores.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Assignments &#151;</FONT></I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> The Company is contingently liable for leases that have been assigned to various third parties in connection with facility closings and dispositions.&nbsp; The Company could be required to satisfy the obligations under the leases if any of the assignees is unable to fulfill its lease obligations.&nbsp; Due to the wide distribution of the Company&#146;s assignments among third parties, and various other remedies available, the Company believes the likelihood that it will be required to assume a material amount of these obligations is remote.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Benefit Plans</FONT></I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> &#151; The Company administers certain non-contributory defined benefit retirement plans and contributory defined contribution retirement plans for substantially all non-union employees and some union-represented employees as determined by the terms and conditions of collective bargaining agreements. Funding for the defined benefit pension plans is based on a review of the specific requirements, and an evaluation of the assets and liabilities, of each plan.&nbsp; Funding for the Company&#146;s matching and automatic contributions under the defined contribution plans is based on years of service, plan compensation, and amount of contributions by participants.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In addition to providing pension benefits, the Company provides certain health care benefits for retired employees. Funding for the retiree health care benefits occurs as claims or premiums are paid.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The determination of the obligation and expense for the Company&#146;s defined benefit retirement pension plan and other post-retirement benefits is dependent on the Company&#146;s selection of assumptions used by actuaries in calculating those amounts. Those assumptions are described in the Company&#146;s 2008 Annual Report on Form&nbsp;10-K and include, among other things, the discount rate, the expected long-term rate of return on plan assets, and the rates of increase in compensation and health care costs. Actual results that differ from the assumptions are accumulated and amortized over future periods and, therefore, generally affect the recognized expense and recorded obligation in such future periods. While the Company believes that the assumptions are appropriate, significant differences in actual experience or significant changes in assumptions may materially affect the pension and other post-retirement obligations and future expense.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2>The Company contributed $200 to its Company-sponsored defined benefit pension plans in the first two quarters of 2009. The Company expects these contributions will reduce its minimum required contributions in future years. Among other things, investment performance of plan assets, the interest rates required to be used to calculate pension obligations and future changes in legislation will determine the amounts of any additional contributions.&nbsp; In addition, the Company expects our cash contributions and expense to the 401(k)&nbsp;Retirement Savings Account Plan from automatic and matching contributions to participants to increase in 2009, compared to 2008.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company also contributes to various multi-employer pension plans based on obligations arising from most of its collective bargaining agreements. These plans provide retirement benefits to participants based on their service to contributing employers. The benefits are paid from assets held in trust for that purpose. Trustees are appointed in equal number by employers and unions. The trustees typically are responsible for determining the level of benefits to be provided to participants as well as for such matters as the investment of the assets and the administration of the plans.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Based on the most recent information available to it, the Company believes that the present value of actuarial accrued liabilities in most or all of these multi-employer plans exceeds the value of the assets held in trust to pay benefits. Because the Company is one of a number of employers contributing to these plans, it is difficult to ascertain what the Company&#146;s &#147;share&#148; of the underfunding would be, although we anticipate the Company&#146;s contributions to these plans will increase each year.&nbsp; The Company believes that funding levels have not changed significantly since year end.&nbsp; As a result, the Company believes its contributions to multi-employer pension plans could as much as double over the next several years, after 2009, to reduce this underfunding.&nbsp; Moreover, if the Company were to exit certain markets or otherwise cease making contributions to these funds, the Company could trigger a substantial withdrawal liability. Any adjustment for withdrawal liability will be recorded when it is probable that a liability exists and can be reasonably determined, in accordance with SFAS No.&nbsp;87,<I> Employers&#146; Accounting for Pensions</I>.</FONT></P></BODY></HTML> <!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN"> <HTML><HEAD> <META http-equiv=Content-Type content="text/html; charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>12.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>D</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ERIVATIVE </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>F</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>INANCIAL</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> I</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>NSTRUMENTS</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>SFAS No.&nbsp;133, <I>Accounting for Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities</I> (SFAS 133), as amended, defines derivatives, requires that derivatives be carried at fair value on the balance sheet and provides for hedge accounting when certain conditions are met.&nbsp; In accordance with this standard, the Company&#146;s derivative financial instruments are recognized on the balance sheet at fair value.&nbsp; Changes in the fair value of derivative instruments designated as &#147;cash flow&#148; hedges, to the extent the hedges are highly effective, are recorded in other comprehensive income, net of tax effects.&nbsp; Ineffective portions of cash flow hedges, if any, are recognized in current period earnings.&nbsp; Other comprehensive income or loss is reclassified into current period earnings when the hedged transaction affects earnings.&nbsp; Changes in the fair value of derivative instruments designated as &#147;fair value&#148; hedges, along with corresponding changes in the fair values of the hedged assets or liabilities, are recorded in current period earnings.&nbsp; Ineffective portions of fair value hedges, if any, are recognized in current period earnings.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company assesses, both at the inception of the hedge and on an ongoing basis, whether derivatives used as hedging instruments are highly effective in offsetting the changes in the fair value or cash flow of the hedged items. &nbsp;If it is determined that a derivative is not highly effective as a hedge or ceases to be highly effective, the Company discontinues hedge accounting prospectively.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Interest Rate Risk Management</FONT></I></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company is exposed to market risk from fluctuations in interest rates.&nbsp; The Company manages its exposure to interest rate fluctuations through the use of interest rate swaps (fair value hedges) and forward-starting interest rate swaps (cash flow hedges).&nbsp; The Company&#146;s current program relative to interest rate protection contemplates hedging the exposure to changes in the fair value of fixed-rate debt attributable to changes in interest rates.&nbsp; To do this, the Company uses the following guidelines: (i)&nbsp;use average daily outstanding borrowings to determine annual debt amounts subject to interest rate exposure, (ii)&nbsp;limit the average annual amount subject to interest rate reset and the amount of floating rate debt to a combined total of $2,500 or less, (iii)&nbsp;include no leverage products, and (iv)&nbsp;hedge without regard to profit motive or sensitivity to current mark-to-market status.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Annually, the Company reviews with the Financial Policy Committee of the Board of Directors compliance with the guidelines.&nbsp; These guidelines may change as the Company&#146;s needs dictate.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><U><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair Value Interest Rate Swaps</FONT></U></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>At the end of the second quarter of 2009, the Company maintained 18 interest rate swap agreements that are being accounted for as fair value hedges.&nbsp; </FONT><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>The gain or loss on these derivative instruments as well as the offsetting gain or loss on the hedged items attributable to the hedged risk are recognized in current income as &#147;Interest expense&#148;.&nbsp; These gains and losses for </FONT><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>the second quarter of 2009 and the first two quarters of 2009, were as follows:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <TABLE style="WIDTH: face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company has unamortized proceeds from twelve interest rate swaps once classified as fair value hedges totaling approximately $36.&nbsp; The unamortized proceeds are recorded as adjustments to the carrying values of the underlying debt and are being amortized over the remaining term of the debt.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company expects to reclassify an unrealized gain of $14 from this adjustment to the carrying values of the underlying debt to earnings over the next twelve months.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-ALIGN: center" align=center><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><U><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Cash Flow Forward-Starting Interest Rate Swaps</FONT></U></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company did not maintain any forward-starting interest rate swap derivatives.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company has unamortized net payments from three forward-starting interest rate swaps once classified as cash flow hedges totaling approximately $12.&nbsp; The unamortized proceeds and payments from these terminated forward-starting interest rate swaps have been recorded net of tax in other comprehensive income and will be amortized to earnings as the payments of interest to which the hedges relate are made.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company expects to reclassify an unrealized net loss of $2 from Accumulated Other Comprehensive Loss (&#147;AOCL&#148;) to earnings over the next twelve months.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT></I>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Commodity Price Protection</FONT></I></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company enters into purchase commitments for various resources, including raw materials utilized in its manufacturing facilities and energy to be used in its stores, warehouses, manufacturing facilities and administrative offices.&nbsp; The Company enters into commitments expecting to take delivery of and to utilize those resources in the conduct of normal business.&nbsp; Those commitments for which the Company expects to utilize or take delivery in a reasonable amount of time in the normal course of business qualify as normal purchases and normal sales.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Some of the product the Company purchases is shipped in corrugated cardboard packaging.&nbsp; The corrugated cardboard is sold when it is economical for the Company to do so.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company maintained a derivative instrument to manage exposure to changes in corrugated cardboard prices.&nbsp; This derivative has a three-year term.&nbsp; The instrument does not qualify for hedge accounting, in accordance with SFAS 133.&nbsp; Accordingly, the change in the fair value of this instrument is marked-to-market in the Company&#146;s Consolidated Statements of Operations as operating, general and administrative expense.&nbsp; For the second quarter of 2009 and the first two quarters of 2009, the change in the fair value of this instrument was insignificant.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the fair value of this instrument was insignificant.</FONT></P></BODY></HTML> <!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN"> <HTML><HEAD> <META http-equiv=Content-Type content="text/html; charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>13.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>F</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>AIR</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> V</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ALUE</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> M</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>EASUREMENTS</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In September&nbsp;2006, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;157, <I>Fair Value Measurements</I> (SFAS 157), which defines fair value, establishes a market-based framework for measuring fair value and expands disclosures about fair value measurements.&nbsp; SFAS 157 does not expand or require any new fair value measurements.&nbsp; SFAS 157 is effective for financial assets and financial liabilities for fiscal years beginning after November&nbsp;15, 2007.&nbsp; FASB Staff Position (FSP) 157-2,<I> Effective Date of Statement No.&nbsp;157</I> (FSP 157-2), deferred the effective date of SFAS No.&nbsp;157 for most non-financial assets and non-financial liabilities to fiscal years beginning after November&nbsp;15, 2008.&nbsp; The Company adopted SFAS 157 for financial assets and financial liabilities effective February&nbsp;3, 2008 and adopted the remaining provisions of SFAS 157 for nonfinancial assets and nonfinancial liabilities on February&nbsp;1, 2009.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>SFAS 157 establishes a fair value hierarchy that prioritizes the inputs used to measure fair value.&nbsp; The three levels of the fair value hierarchy defined by SFAS 157 are as follows:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 1 &#150; Quoted prices are available in active markets for identical assets or liabilities;</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 2 &#150; Pricing inputs are other than quoted prices in active markets included in Level 1, which are either directly or indirectly observable;</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 3 &#150; Unobservable pricing inputs in which little or no market activity exists, therefore requiring an entity to develop its own assumptions about the assumptions that market participants would use in pricing an asset or liability.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>For those financial instruments carried at fair value in the consolidated financial statements, the following table summarizes the fair value of these instruments at August&nbsp;15, 2009:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in; TEXT-ALIGN: center" align=center><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair Value Measurements Using</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <TABLE style="WIDTH: 100%; BORDER-COLLAPSE: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair value measurements of nonfinancial assets and nonfinancial liabilities are primarily used in the impairment analysis of goodwill, other intangible assets, and long-lived assets and in the valuation of store lease exit costs.&nbsp; The Company reviews goodwill and other intangible assets for impairment annually, during the fourth quarter of each fiscal year, or as circumstances indicate the possibility of impairment in accordance with SFAS No.&nbsp;142, <I>Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets</I>.&nbsp; Long-lived assets and store lease exit costs were measured at fair value on a nonrecurring basis using Level 3 inputs as defined in the fair value hierarchy.&nbsp; See Note 1 for further discussion of the Company&#146;s policies and recorded amounts for impairments of long-lived assets and valuation of store lease exit costs.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; estimated based on the quoted market price for the same or similar issues adjusted for illiquidity based on available market evidence.&nbsp; If quoted market prices were not available, the fair value was based upon the net present value of the future cash flow using the forward interest rate yield curve in effect at August&nbsp;15, 2009.&nbsp; At August&nbsp;15, 2009, the fair value of total debt was $7,804 compared to a carrying value of $7,074.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" including noncontrolling interests Net earnings attributable to Horizon Foods Group Document and Entity Information NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS Deposits In-Transit Deposits in-transit Generally represents funds deposited to bank accounts, as of the balance sheet date, related to sales, a majority of which were paid for with credit cards and checks, to which the depositor does not have immediate access. Prefunded Employee Benefits Prefunded employee benefits Amount represents prepaid funds, as of the balance sheet date, in a Voluntary Employee Benefit Arrangement, used to pay employee medical expenses. Prepaid Expenses and Other Assets, Current Prepaid and other current assets Aggregate carrying amounts, as of the balance sheet date, of expenditures made in advance of the timing of recognition of expenses which are expected to be charged against earnings within one year or the normal operating cycle, if longer, and of current assets not separately disclosed in the balance sheet due to materiality considerations, which are expected to be realized or consumed within one year (or the normal operating cycle, if longer). Operating, General and Administrative Expense Operating, general and administrative The aggregate total of expenses of managing and administering the affairs of an entity, including affiliates of the reporting entity, which are not directly or indirectly associated with the manufacture, sale or creation of a product or product line, excluding rental expense incurred for leased assets including furniture and equipment which has not been recognized in costs and expenses applicable to sales and revenues; for example, cost of goods sold or other operating costs and expenses. Increase (Decrease) in Store Deposits In-Transit Deposits in-transit The net change during the reporting period in the amount of funds deposited to bank accounts related to sales, a majority of which were paid for with credit cards and checks, to which the depositor does not have immediate access. Payments on credit facility Borrowings Payments on Bank Revolver The cash inflow or outflow related specifically to borrowings under a Credit Facility, money market lines and commercial paper. Reconciliation of Capital Expenditures [Abstract] Reconciliation of capital expenditures: Capital Expenditures Total capital expenditures The cash outflow for purchases of and capital improvements on property, plant and equipment (capital expenditures), software, and other intangible assets, plus net change in construction in progress payables during the reporting period. Issuance of common stock: Treasury stock activity: Stock Options and Restricted Stock Exchanged, Shares Stock options exchanged (in shares) Number of shares that have been repurchased during the period using proceeds received from exercises of stock options and the tax benefits associated therewith. Variable Interest Entity, Net Cash Inflows (Outflows) Cash from Consolidated Variable Interest Entity Represents the inflow of cash due to the consolidation of a variable interest entity. These amounts were the initial investment in or loan to a consolidated variable interest entity, recognized upon consolidation of the variable interest entity. Long-Term Debt Including Obligations under Capital Leases and Financing Obligations Long-term debt including obligations under capital leases and financing obligations Sum of the carrying values as of the balance sheet date of all long-term debt, which is debt initially having maturities due after one year from the balance sheet date or beyond the operating cycle, if longer, but excluding the portions thereof scheduled to be repaid within one year or the normal operating cycle, if longer plus capital lease obligations due to be paid more than one year after the balance sheet date, plus unamortized proceeds from discontinued fair value hedges and the fair value, as of the balance sheet date, of all derivatives designated as interest rate fair value hedging instruments. LIFO Charge LIFO charge Net change in LIFO Inventory reserve, which has no impact on cash. Changes in construction-in-progress payables Increase (Decrease) in Construction in Progress Payables Net change in construction in progress payables during the reporting period. Adjustment to reflect fair-value interest rate hedges Adjustment to Reflect Fair Value Interest Rate Hedges Represents unamortized proceeds from discontinued fair value hedges and the fair value, as of the balance sheet date, of all derivatives designated as interest rate fair value hedging instruments. Expense for Company-sponsored pension plans Represents the amount recognized for certain defined benefit pension benefit costs during the period. Pension expense includes the following components: service cost, interest cost, expected return on plan assets, gain or loss on plan assets, prior service cost or credit, transition asset or obligation, and gain or loss due to settlements or curtailments. Pension Expense, Defined Benefit Plans Stock Options and Restricted Stock Exchanged, Value Stock options exchanged Stock options exchanged Cost of common stock repurchased during the period using proceeds received from exercises of stock options and the tax benefits associated therewith. Recorded using the cost method. Recently Issued Accounting Standards [Text Block] RECENTLY ISSUED ACCOUNTING STANDARDS Disclosure of new accounting pronouncements that have been issued but not yet adopted, including description of (1) the new pronouncement, the date that adoption is required and the date that the entity plans to adopt, if earlier; (2) the methods of adoption allowed by the pronouncement and the method expected to be utilized by the entity, if determined; (3) the impact that adoption of the pronouncement is expected to have on the financial statements of the entity, unless such impact is not known or reasonably estimable (in which case, a statement to that effect should be made) and; (4) the potential impact of other significant matters that the entity believes might result from the adoption of the pronouncement (for exa mple, technical violations of debt covenant agreements and planned or intended changes in business practices.) Purchase of Non-Wholly Owned Entity Purchase of non-wholly owned entity The amount recorded for noncontrolling interest due to the purchase of a non-wholly owned entity. Restricted Stock Awards, Value Restricted stock issued Value of common stock surrendered / repurchased during the period to withhold for employee taxes due to the lapsing and issuance of restricted stock from common stock and treasury stock, respectively. Restricted Stock Awards, Shares Restricted stock issued (in shares) Shares of common stock surrendered / repurchased during the period to withhold for employee taxes due to the lapsing and issuance of restricted stock from common stock and treasury stock, respectively. Stockholders' Equity, Period Increase (Decrease) Stock Issued During Period, Shares, Period Increase (Decrease)

charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>13.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>F</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>AIR</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> V</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ALUE</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> M</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>EASUREMENTS</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In September&nbsp;2006, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;157, <I>Fair Value Measurements</I> (SFAS 157), which defines fair value, establishes a market-based framework for measuring fair value and expands disclosures about fair value measurements.&nbsp; SFAS 157 does not expand or require any new fair value measurements.&nbsp; SFAS 157 is effective for financial assets and financial liabilities for fiscal years beginning after November&nbsp;15, 2007.&nbsp; FASB Staff Position (FSP) 157-2,<I> Effective Date of Statement No.&nbsp;157</I> (FSP 157-2), deferred the effective date of SFAS No.&nbsp;157 for most non-financial assets and non-financial liabilities to fiscal years beginning after November&nbsp;15, 2008.&nbsp; The Company adopted SFAS 157 for financial assets and financial liabilities effective February&nbsp;3, 2008 and adopted the remaining provisions of SFAS 157 for nonfinancial assets and nonfinancial liabilities on February&nbsp;1, 2009.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>SFAS 157 establishes a fair value hierarchy that prioritizes the inputs used to measure fair value.&nbsp; The three levels of the fair value hierarchy defined by SFAS 157 are as follows:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 1 &#150; Quoted prices are available in active markets for identical assets or liabilities;</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 2 &#150; Pricing inputs are other than quoted prices in active markets included in Level 1, which are either directly or indirectly observable;</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Level 3 &#150; Unobservable pricing inputs in which little or no market activity exists, therefore requiring an entity to develop its own assumptions about the assumptions that market participants would use in pricing an asset or liability.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>For those financial instruments carried at fair value in the consolidated financial statements, the following table summarizes the fair value of these instruments at August&nbsp;15, 2009:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in; TEXT-ALIGN: center" align=center><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair Value Measurements Using</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <TABLE style="WIDTH: 100%; BORDER-COLLAPSE: collapse" cellSpacing=0 cellPadding=0 width="100%" border=0> <TBODY> <TR> <TD style="PADDING-RIGHT: 0in; PADDING-LEFT: 0in; PADDING-BOTTOM: 0in; WIDTH: 33%; PADDING-TOP: 0in" vAlign=bottom width="33%"> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 10pt; TEXT-INDENT: -10pt"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 1pt" face="Times New Roman" size=1></FONT></B>&nbsp;</P></TD> <TD style="PADDING-RIGHT: 0in; PADDING-LEFT: 0in; PADDING-BOTTOM: 0in; WIDTH: 2.5%; PADDING-TOP: 0in" vAlign=bottom width="2%"> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-ALIGN: center" align=center><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 1pt" face="Times New Roman" size=1></FONT></B>&nbsp;</P></TD> <TD style="BORDER-RIGHT: medium none; PADDING-RIGHT: 0in; BORDER-TOP: medium none; PADDING-LEFT: 0in; PADDING-BOTTOM: 0in; BORDER-LEFT: medium none; WIDTH: 14%; PADDING-TOP: 0in; BORDER-BOTTOM: windowtext 1pt solid" vAlign=bottom width="14%" colSpan=2> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair value measurements of nonfinancial assets and nonfinancial liabilities are primarily used in the impairment analysis of goodwill, other intangible assets, and long-lived assets and in the valuation of store lease exit costs.&nbsp; The Company reviews goodwill and other intangible assets for impairment annually, during the fourth quarter of each fiscal year, or as circumstances indicate the possibility of impairment in accordance with SFAS No.&nbsp;142, <I>Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets</I>.&nbsp; Long-lived assets and store lease exit costs were measured at fair value on a nonrecurring basis using Level 3 inputs as defined in the fair value hierarchy.&nbsp; See Note 1 for further discussion of the Company&#146;s policies and recorded amounts for impairments of long-lived assets and valuation of store lease exit costs.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair Value of Other Financial Instruments</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; PAGE-BREAK-AFTER: auto; COLOR: black; TEXT-ALIGN: left" align=left><B><I><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: normal; FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2>Current and Long-term Debt</FONT></I></B></P> <P style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; PAGE-BREAK-AFTER: auto; COLOR: black; TEXT-ALIGN: left" align=left><B><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT></B>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2>The fair value of the Company&#146;s long-term debt, including current maturities, was estimated based on the quoted market price for the same or similar issues adjusted for illiquidity based on available market evidence.&nbsp; If quoted market prices were not available, the fair value was based upon the net present value of the future cash flow using the forward interest rate yield curve in effect at August&nbsp;15, 2009.&nbsp; At August&nbsp;15, 2009, the fair value of total debt was $7,804 compared to a carrying value of $7,074.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 9pt"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Cash and Temporary Cash Investments, Store Deposits In-Transit, Receivables, Prepaid and Other Current Assets, Accounts Payable, Accrued Salaries and Wages and Other Current Liabilities</FONT></I></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>S</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company recognized total stock-based compensation of $20 and $23 in the second quarter ended August&nbsp;15, 2009 and August&nbsp;16, 2008, respectively.&nbsp; The Company recorded $45 and $48 of stock-based compensation for the first two quarters ended August&nbsp;15, 2009 and August&nbsp;16, 2008, respectively.&nbsp; These costs were recognized as operating, general and administrative costs in the Company&#146;s Consolidated Statements of Operations.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company grants options for common stock (&#147;stock options&#148;) to employees, as well as to its non-employee directors, under various plans at an option price equal to the fair market value of the stock at the date of grant. In addition to stock options, the Company awards restricted stock to employees and its non-employee directors under various plans.&nbsp; Equity awards may be made once each quarter on a predetermined date.&nbsp; It has been the Company&#146;s practice to make a general annual grant to employees, which occurred in the second quarter of 2009.&nbsp; Special grants may be made in the other three quarters.&nbsp; It has been the Company&#146;s practice to make a grant to non-employee directors in December&nbsp;of each year.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Stock options granted in the first two quarters of 2009 expire 10 years from the date of grant and vest from one year to five years from the date of grant. Restricted stock awards granted in the first two quarters of 2009 have restrictions that lapse in one year to five years from the date of the awards. All grants and awards become immediately exercisable, in the case of options, and restrictions lapse, in the case of restricted stock, upon certain changes of control of the Company.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2>Changes in equity awards outstanding under the plans are summarized below.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; COLOR: windowtext" face="Times New Roman" color=black size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; PAGE-BREAK-AFTER: auto; COLOR: black; TEXT-INDENT: 9pt; FONT-STYLE: italic"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The weighted-average fair value of stock options granted during the first two quarters ended August&nbsp;15, 2009 and August&nbsp;16, 2008, was $6.30 and $8.67, respectively. The fair value of each stock option grant was estimated on the date of grant using the Black-Scholes option-pricing model, based on the assumptions shown in the table below. The Black-Scholes model utilizes extensive accounting judgment and financial estimates, including the term employees are expected to retain their stock options before exercising them, the volatility of the Company&#146;s stock price over that expected term, the dividend yield over the term, and the number of awards expected to be forfeited before they vest. Using alternative assumptions in the calculation of fair value would produce fair values for stock option grants that could be different than those used to record stock-based compensation expense in the Consolidated Statements of Operations.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The following table reflects the weighted average assumptions used for grants awarded to option holders:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; 1.0.0.3 false DERIVATIVE FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS false 1 $ false false shares Standard http://www.xbrl.org/2003/instance shares xbrli 0 USD Standard http://www.xbrl.org/2003/iso4217 USD iso4217 0 USDPerShare Divide http://www.xbrl.org/2003/iso4217 USD iso4217 http://www.xbrl.org/2003/instance shares xbrli 0 2 NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS 0 kr_NotesToConsolidatedFinancialsAbstract kr false na duration string No definition available. false false false false false true false false false 1 false false 0 0 false false No definition available. false 3 DERIVATIVE FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS 1 us-gaap_DerivativeInstrumentsAndHedgingActivitiesDisclosureTextBlock us-gaap true na duration string No definition available. false false false false false false false false false 1 false false 0 0 <!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN"> <HTML><HEAD> <META http-equiv=Content-Type content="text/html; charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>12.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>D</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ERIVATIVE </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>F</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>INANCIAL</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> I</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>NSTRUMENTS</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>SFAS No.&nbsp;133, <I>Accounting for Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities</I> (SFAS 133), as amended, defines derivatives, requires that derivatives be carried at fair value on the balance sheet and provides for hedge accounting when certain conditions are met.&nbsp; In accordance with this standard, the Company&#146;s derivative financial instruments are recognized on the balance sheet at fair value.&nbsp; Changes in the fair value of derivative instruments designated as &#147;cash flow&#148; hedges, to the extent the hedges are highly effective, are recorded in other comprehensive income, net of tax effects.&nbsp; Ineffective portions of cash flow hedges, if any, are recognized in current period earnings.&nbsp; Other comprehensive income or loss is reclassified into current period earnings when the hedged transaction affects earnings.&nbsp; Changes in the fair value of derivative instruments designated as &#147;fair value&#148; hedges, along with corresponding changes in the fair values of the hedged assets or liabilities, are recorded in current period earnings.&nbsp; Ineffective portions of fair value hedges, if any, are recognized in current period earnings.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company assesses, both at the inception of the hedge and on an ongoing basis, whether derivatives used as hedging instruments are highly effective in offsetting the changes in the fair value or cash flow of the hedged items. &nbsp;If it is determined that a derivative is not highly effective as a hedge or ceases to be highly effective, the Company discontinues hedge accounting prospectively.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Interest Rate Risk Management</FONT></I></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company is exposed to market risk from fluctuations in interest rates.&nbsp; The Company manages its exposure to interest rate fluctuations through the use of interest rate swaps (fair value hedges) and forward-starting interest rate swaps (cash flow hedges).&nbsp; The Company&#146;s current program relative to interest rate protection contemplates hedging the exposure to changes in the fair value of fixed-rate debt attributable to changes in interest rates.&nbsp; To do this, the Company uses the following guidelines: (i)&nbsp;use average daily outstanding borrowings to determine annual debt amounts subject to interest rate exposure, (ii)&nbsp;limit the average annual amount subject to interest rate reset and the amount of floating rate debt to a combined total of $2,500 or less, (iii)&nbsp;include no leverage products, and (iv)&nbsp;hedge without regard to profit motive or sensitivity to current mark-to-market status.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Annually, the Company reviews with the Financial Policy Committee of the Board of Directors compliance with the guidelines.&nbsp; These guidelines may change as the Company&#146;s needs dictate.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><U><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Fair Value Interest Rate Swaps</FONT></U></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>At the end of the second quarter of 2009, the Company maintained 18 interest rate swap agreements that are being accounted for as fair value hedges.&nbsp; </FONT><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>The gain or loss on these derivative instruments as well as the offsetting gain or loss on the hedged items attributable to the hedged risk are recognized in current income as &#147;Interest expense&#148;.&nbsp; These gains and losses for </FONT><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>the second quarter of 2009 and the first two quarters of 2009, were as follows:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <TABLE style="WIDTH: 100%; TEXT-INDENT: -10pt"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 1pt" face="Times New Roman" size=1></FONT></B>&nbsp;</P></TD> <TD style="PADDING-RIGHT: 0in; PADDING-LEFT: 0in; PADDING-BOTTOM: 0in; WIDTH: 2.5%; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company has unamortized proceeds from twelve interest rate swaps once classified as fair value hedges totaling approximately $36.&nbsp; The unamortized proceeds are recorded as adjustments to the carrying values of the underlying debt and are being amortized over the remaining term of the debt.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company expects to reclassify an unrealized gain of $14 from this adjustment to the carrying values of the underlying debt to earnings over the next twelve months.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-ALIGN: center" align=center><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><U><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Cash Flow Forward-Starting Interest Rate Swaps</FONT></U></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company did not maintain any forward-starting interest rate swap derivatives.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company has unamortized net payments from three forward-starting interest rate swaps once classified as cash flow hedges totaling approximately $12.&nbsp; The unamortized proceeds and payments from these terminated forward-starting interest rate swaps have been recorded net of tax in other comprehensive income and will be amortized to earnings as the payments of interest to which the hedges relate are made.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company expects to reclassify an unrealized net loss of $2 from Accumulated Other Comprehensive Loss (&#147;AOCL&#148;) to earnings over the next twelve months.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The following table summarizes the effect of the Company&#146;s derivative instruments designated as cash flow hedges for the second quarter of 2009:</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT></I>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt"><I><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt; FONT-STYLE: italic" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Commodity Price Protection</FONT></I></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>The Company enters into purchase commitments for various resources, including raw materials utilized in its manufacturing facilities and energy to be used in its stores, warehouses, manufacturing facilities and administrative offices.&nbsp; The Company enters into commitments expecting to take delivery of and to utilize those resources in the conduct of normal business.&nbsp; Those commitments for which the Company expects to utilize or take delivery in a reasonable amount of time in the normal course of business qualify as normal purchases and normal sales.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Some of the product the Company purchases is shipped in corrugated cardboard packaging.&nbsp; The corrugated cardboard is sold when it is economical for the Company to do so.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the Company maintained a derivative instrument to manage exposure to changes in corrugated cardboard prices.&nbsp; This derivative has a three-year term.&nbsp; The instrument does not qualify for hedge accounting, in accordance with SFAS 133.&nbsp; Accordingly, the change in the fair value of this instrument is marked-to-market in the Company&#146;s Consolidated Statements of Operations as operating, general and administrative expense.&nbsp; For the second quarter of 2009 and the first two quarters of 2009, the change in the fair value of this instrument was insignificant.&nbsp; As of August&nbsp;15, 2009, the fair value of this instrument was insignificant.</FONT></P></BODY></HTML> 12.&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; charset=utf-8"> <META content="MSHTML 6.00.6000.16890" name=GENERATOR></HEAD> <BODY> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>8.</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 3pt" size=1>&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; </FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>R</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>ECENTLY</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> A</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>DOPTED</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> A</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>CCOUNTING</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2> S</FONT></B><B><FONT style="FONT-WEIGHT: bold; FONT-SIZE: 10pt" size=2>TANDARDS</FONT></B></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt 0.25in; TEXT-INDENT: -0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In December&nbsp;2007, the FASB issued SFAS 160, which establishes accounting and reporting standards for a parent&#146;s noncontrolling interest in a subsidiary and the accounting for future ownership changes with respect to the subsidiary.&nbsp; This standard defines a noncontrolling interest, previously called a minority interest, as the portion of equity in a subsidiary that is not attributable, directly or indirectly, to a parent.&nbsp; SFAS 160 requires, among other things, that a noncontrolling interest be clearly identified, labeled and presented in the consolidated balance sheet as equity, but separate from the parent&#146;s equity; that the amount of consolidated net income attributable to the parent and to the noncontrolling interest be clearly identified and presented on the face of the consolidated statement of income; and that if a subsidiary is deconsolidated, the parent measure at fair value any noncontrolling equity investment that the parent retains in the former subsidiary and recognize a gain or loss in net income based on the fair value of the non-controlling equity investment.&nbsp; The Company adopted SFAS 160 effective February&nbsp;1, 2009, and applied it retrospectively.&nbsp; As a result, the Company reclassified noncontrolling interests in amounts of $95 from the mezzanine section to equity in the January&nbsp;31, 2009 Consolidated Balance Sheet.&nbsp; Certain reclassifications to the Consolidated Statement of Operations have been made to prior period amounts to conform to the presentation of the current period under SFAS 160.&nbsp; Recorded amounts for prior periods previously presented as Net Earnings, which are now presented as Net Earnings Attributable to Horizon Foods Group, have not changed as a result of the adoption of SFAS 160.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective February&nbsp;1, 2009, the Company adopted FSP No.&nbsp;FAS 157-2, <I>Effective Date of Statement No.&nbsp;157 </I>(FSP 157-2).&nbsp; FSP 157-2 deferred the effective date of SFAS No.&nbsp;157, <I>Fair Value Measurements</I>, for most non-financial assets and non-financial liabilities to fiscal years beginning after November&nbsp;15, 2008.&nbsp; See Note 13 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for further discussion of the adoption of FSP 157-2.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective February&nbsp;1, 2009, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;141 (Revised 2007), <I>Business Combinations </I>(SFAS 141R), which replaces SFAS No.&nbsp;141<I>.&nbsp; </I>SFAS 141R further expands the definitions of a business and the fair value measurement and reporting in a business combination.&nbsp; All business combinations completed after February&nbsp;1, 2009, will be accounted for under SFAS 141R.&nbsp; The Company did not complete any business combinations during the first two quarters of fiscal 2009.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective February&nbsp;1, 2009, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;161, <I>Disclosures about Derivative Instruments and Hedging Activities-an amendment of FASB Statement No.&nbsp;133 </I>(SFAS 161)<I>.&nbsp; </I>SFAS 161 requires enhanced disclosures on an entity&#146;s derivative and hedging activities.&nbsp; The new disclosures required by this standard are included in Note 12 to the Consolidated Financial Statements.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective February&nbsp;1, 2009, the Company adopted FSP No.&nbsp;EITF 03-6-1, <I>Determining Whether Instruments Granted in Share-Based Payment Transactions Are Participating Securities</I>.<I>&nbsp;&nbsp; </I>This clarifies that share-based payment awards that entitle their holders to receive nonforfeitable dividends before vesting should be considered participating securities and included in the computation of EPS pursuant to the two-class method.&nbsp; See Note 7 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for further discussion of its adoption.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective May&nbsp;24, 2009, the Company adopted SFAS No.&nbsp;165, <I>Subsequent Events</I> (SFAS 165).&nbsp; SFAS 165 is intended to establish general standards of accounting for and disclosure of events that occur after the balance sheet date but before financial statements are issued or are available to be issued.&nbsp; SFAS 165 requires the disclosure of the date through which an entity has evaluated subsequent events and the basis for that date.&nbsp; See Note 14 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for further discussion of its adoption.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>Effective May&nbsp;24, 2009, the Company adopted three new FASB Staff Positions (FSP) all of which impact the accounting and disclosure related to certain financial instruments.&nbsp; FSP No.&nbsp;FAS 157-4, <I>Determining Fair Value When the Volume and Level of Activity for the Asset or Liability Have Significantly Decreased and Identifying Transactions That Are Not Orderly</I>, provides additional guidance for estimating fair value in accordance with SFAS 157 when the volume and level of activity for the asset or liability have significantly decreased.&nbsp; It also includes guidance on identifying circumstances that indicate a transaction is not orderly.&nbsp; FSP No.&nbsp;FAS 115-2 and FAS 124-2, <I>Recognition and Presentation of Other-Than-Temporary Impairments</I>, amends the other-than-temporary impairment guidance for debt securities to make the guidance more operational and to improve the presentation and disclosure of other-than-temporary impairments on debt and equity securities in the financial statements.&nbsp; FSP No.&nbsp;FAS 107-1 and APB 28-1, <I>Interim Disclosures about Fair Value of Financial Instruments</I>, amends SFAS No.&nbsp;107, <I>Disclosures about Fair Value of Financial Instruments, </I>to require disclosures about the fair value of financial instruments on an interim basis in addition to the annual disclosure requirements.&nbsp; The new disclosures required by this FSP are included in Note 13.&nbsp; The adoption of these FSP&#146;s did not have a material effect on the Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial Statements.</FONT></P></BODY></HTML> 8.&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In December&nbsp;2008, the FASB issued FSP No.&nbsp;FAS 132(R)-1, <I>Employers&#146; Disclosure about Postretirement Benefit Plan Assets</I> (FSP 132(R)-1).&nbsp; FSP 132(R)-1 provides additional guidance on employers&#146; disclosures about the plan assets of defined benefit pension or other postretirement plans.&nbsp; FSP 132(R)-1 requires disclosures about how investment allocation decisions are made, the fair value of each major category of plan assets, valuation techniques used to develop fair value measurements of plan assets, the impact of measurements on changes in plan assets when using significant unobservable inputs and significant concentrations of risk in the plan assets.&nbsp; FSP 132(R)-1 becomes effective for fiscal years ending after December&nbsp;15, 2009.&nbsp; The Company is currently evaluating the effect the adoption of FSP 132(R)-1 will have on its Consolidated Financial Statements.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In June&nbsp;2009, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;167, <I>Amendments to FASB Interpretation No.&nbsp;46(R)&nbsp;</I>(SFAS 167).&nbsp; SFAS 167 changes how a company determines when an entity that is insufficiently capitalized or is not controlled through voting (or similar rights) should be consolidated.&nbsp; SFAS 167 will become effective for the Company&#146;s fiscal year beginning January&nbsp;31, 2010.&nbsp; The Company is currently evaluating the effect the adoption of SFAS 167 will have on its Consolidated Financial Statements.</FONT></P> <P style="MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2></FONT>&nbsp;</P> <P style="BACKGROUND: white; MARGIN: 0in 0in 0pt; TEXT-INDENT: 0.25in"><FONT style="FONT-SIZE: 10pt" face="Times New Roman" size=2>In July&nbsp;2009, the FASB issued SFAS No.&nbsp;168, <I><FONT style="FONT-STYLE: italic">The </FONT>FASB Accounting Standards Codification&#153; and the Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles&#151;a replacement of FASB Statement No.&nbsp;162</I> (SFAS 168), which is effective for interim and annual fiscal periods ending after September&nbsp;15, 2009.&nbsp; This standard replaces SFAS No.&nbsp;162, <I>The Hierarchy of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles</I>, and the codification from this standard will supersede all then-existing non-SEC accounting and reporting standards to become the sole source of authoritative U.S. GAAP.&nbsp; The adoption of this standard will have no financial effect on the Company&#146;s Consolidated Financial Statements.</FONT></P></BODY></HTML> 9.&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; RECENTLY ISSUED ACCOUNTING STANDARDS &nbsp; In December&nbsp;2008, the FASB false false Disclosure of new accounting pronouncements that have been issued but not yet adopted, including description of (1) the new pronouncement, the date that adoption is required and the date that the entity plans to adopt, if earlier; (2) the methods of adoption allowed by the pronouncement and the method expected to be utilized by the entity, if determined; (3) the impact that adoption of the pronouncement is expected to have on the financial statements of the entity, unless such impact is not known or reasonably estimable (in which case, a statement to that effect should be made) and; (4) the potential impact of other significant matters that the entity believes might result from the adoption of the pronouncement (for example, technical violations of debt covenant agreements and planned or intended changes in business practices.)
